text:
I've read the vocabulary, items, and copy fixtures. The vocabulary offers `metric-tile` (label, value, unit?, tone?) as the library-generic KPI primitive — exactly what an insight-panel of vertical metric tiles needs; other components (`plant-card`, `care-task-row`, `stat-tile`, `status-chip`, `screen-header`, `primary-button`, `demo-notice`) belong to the Verdant plant scenario or app chrome, not this stock-oversell panel, so I will use **only `metric-tile`**, repeated 5 times in an array (no children needed — metric-tile allows none).

From `items.json` (22 SKUs), I hand-computed which rows are oversold (`committed > onHand`) and their shortfall (`committed - onHand`):
- sku-03 Pallet wrap, 23 micron — east — onHand 60, committed 145 → shortfall 85
- sku-10 Stretch film, hand 400% — east — onHand 90, committed 130 → shortfall 40
- sku-16 Wooden pallet, EUR — west — onHand 340, committed 410 → shortfall 70

That's 3 oversold SKUs total, summed shortfall = 85+40+70 = 195 units. By warehouse, oversold SKUs cluster in **east** (2 of the 3, deepest shortfall too), vs west (1), north/south (0) — so exposure concentrates in east. I also counted `status: "low"` rows (thin cover, not yet oversold but at risk of tipping) — sku-07, sku-12, sku-14, sku-19, sku-21 = 5 SKUs.

Planned five `metric-tile` nodes, top to bottom (worst/most urgent first):
1. label "Oversold SKUs" · value "3" · unit "SKUs" · tone critical — how many SKUs are in the oversold state right now
2. label "Total shortfall across oversold SKUs" · value "195" · unit "units" · tone critical — aggregate depth of the hole
3. label "Deepest single shortfall — Pallet wrap, 23 micron" · value "85" · unit "units" · tone critical — where it bites hardest (single worst SKU)
4. label "Most exposed warehouse — East" · value "2" · unit "oversold SKUs" · tone warn — concentration by location
5. label "Thin-cover SKUs (low, at risk)" · value "5" · unit "SKUs" · tone warn — near-term pipeline of the next oversells

This directly answers "where is oversell exposure concentrated (east warehouse, one SKU carrying the deepest hole) and how deep (195 units total, 85 at the worst point)."

Re-reading the vocabulary file to adversarially double-check the exact prop names/types/enums for `metric-tile` before writing anything.

Verified: `metric-tile` has exactly four props — `label` (string, required), `value` (string, required), `unit` (string, optional), `tone` (enum: neutral/warn/critical, optional) — and `children: []` (no children allowed, so plain leaf nodes). My planned five nodes use only these props, all values as strings (not numbers), and both tones I planned (`critical`, `warn`) are in the enum. Five nodes is within the insight-panel's stated 3–6 bound, and it's a flat vertical array as required — no illegal children, no cross-scenario components (`plant-card`, `care-task-row`, etc. correctly excluded). Checking labels again against the tone-redundancy rule: each of the five reads the concentration/depth fact plainly (SKU count, unit total, named SKU + shortfall, named warehouse + count, thin-cover count) without leaning on tone alone — tone only adds emphasis on top of an already-legible label+value pair. Nothing to correct; proceeding to write.

Writing the five-tile composition now, ordered worst-first (oversold count → total depth → single deepest hole → geographic concentration → near-term risk pipeline).
Running the exact validation command named in the task against the real vocabulary.

The validator confirms the composition is structurally sound against the vocabulary. The final panel — five `metric-tile` nodes, all vertically stacked as the insight-panel slot requires — reports: 3 oversold SKUs, 195 total shortfall units, the single deepest hole (Pallet wrap, 23 micron, −85 units), the geographic concentration (east warehouse carries 2 of the 3 oversells), and the 5 thin-cover SKUs likely to tip next. Every figure was hand-computed from `items.json`'s `onHand`/`committed` fields, no invented numbers, and labels carry the SKU/warehouse names while values stay pure numbers per the headline-figure rule.
